policy_statement: Maintain the City’s climate protection goal to become carbon neutral by 2050 (Resolution 31312).
source_quote: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council adopted a bold climate protection goal for our community to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312). The resulting 2013 Climate Action Plan (CAP) provided a coordinated strategy aimed at reducing GHG emissions while also supporting other community goals, including building vibrant neighborhoods, fostering economic prosperity, and enhancing racial and social justice.
extraction_rationale: This is a quantified, time-bound community-wide target adopted by governing bodies (Mayor and City Council) via a cited resolution, which qualifies as a policy commitment even though implementation details are not enumerated in this excerpt.

policy_statement: Affirm Seattle’s commitment to the goals of the Paris Agreement and direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify actions necessary to limit warming to 1.5°C (Resolution 31757).
source_quote: In response to the Trump administration's withdrawal of support for the international Paris Climate Agreement last year, the City Council adopted Resolution 31757, affirming Seattle's commitment to the goals established in the Paris Agreement, and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify the actions necessary to do our part to limit warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius. The resulting actions, developed under the leadership of Mayor Durkan, reflect a tipping point in the transition to Seattle's zero emissions future.
extraction_rationale: A City Council resolution that both affirms a climate commitment and directs a City office to take specific follow-on action (identify necessary actions), meeting the binding mechanism criterion.

policy_statement: Reduce passenger transportation emissions 82% by 2030 (stated city goal).
source_quote: Passenger transportation emissions declined an average of 0.5 percent per year since 2008. To achieve the 2030 goal of reducing passenger transportation emissions 82 percent, Seattle would have needed to reduce emissions from passenger vehicles an average of 7.5 percent each year from 2008 onwards.
extraction_rationale: This is a quantified, time-bound emissions-reduction target (82% by 2030) referenced as an established city goal, qualifying as a policy target even though the excerpt discusses progress rather than adopting the goal.

policy_statement: Reduce building emissions 39% by 2030 (stated city goal).
source_quote: Building emissions declined an average of 2.3 percent per year since 2008, which is on track to meet the goal of reducing emissions 39 percent by 2030 (2.2 percent per year). However, between 2012 and 2014 the pace of building emissions reductions has slowed to an average of 2.1 percent, and an analysis of anticipated reductions based on a current business-as-usual approach shows emissions in 2050 only 12 percent below 2008 levels.
extraction_rationale: This is a quantified, time-bound emissions-reduction target (39% by 2030) referenced as an established goal; it qualifies as a policy target despite being described in a performance/progress context.

policy_statement: Permit installation of publicly available EV charging stations in the right-of-way on non-residential streets via a 2017 pilot program.
source_quote: Visible and ready access to charging infrastructure is essential to expanding vehicle electrification. To meet that need, in 2017, a pilot program was initiated to permit the installation of publicly-available electric vehicle charging stations on non-residential streets in urban villages, urban centers, and commercial streets. To date, over 60 applications have been received from three applicants, including Seattle City Light, who installed the first station in February 2018.
section_header: ## Electric Vehicle Charging in the Right of Way
extraction_rationale: A specific named intervention (pilot permitting program) that changes how right-of-way is used to enable EV charging; this is deliberate government action rather than background.

policy_statement: Implement Seattle City Light EV charging infrastructure pilot programs, including installing 20 public fast charging stations.
source_quote: In response to customers' increasing desire for electric vehicles and to understand how best to manage the impact of vehicle charging on the electrical grid, Seattle City Light is designing and implementing two charging infrastructure pilot programs. The programs will expand public fast charging availability and support residential charging at home. Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city.
extraction_rationale: A specific utility intervention with quantified deployment (20 fast charging stations), meeting the named intervention/quantified target criteria.

policy_statement: Amend the Seattle Electrical Code to require EV-ready electrical capacity in new parking (capacity for one EV charging station per parking space) and documentation for future conduit/equipment locations.
source_quote: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and that construction documents show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of electric vehicles charging stations.
extraction_rationale: A binding regulatory code amendment with explicit technical requirements (one EV charging station per space capacity) that qualifies as a policy mechanism.

policy_statement: Develop a dense network of shared mobility hubs co-located with major transit facilities, including accommodating fast charging to advance electric car share and ride-hail vehicles.
source_quote: Seattle's Shared Mobility Hub program will aggregate transportation connections, travel information, and other mobility amenities into a seamless, understandable, and on-demand travel experience. The Seattle Transportation Department is developing a dense network of shared mobility hubs throughout the city, co-located with major transit facilities and in places where frequent transit services intersect. A key objective for shared mobility hubs is to advance the use of electric car share and ride hail vehicles by accommodating fast charging at or very near hub locations.
extraction_rationale: A named program with a concrete planned intervention (developing a dense network of hubs) and explicit EV infrastructure component, meeting the named intervention criterion.

policy_statement: Reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50% by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels where needed, operational efficiency, and procurement standards (Green Fleet Action Plan).
source_quote: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement. The City's fleet includes over 200 electric vehicles and over 300 hybrid vehicles.
extraction_rationale: Quantified, time-bound target (50% by 2025) plus named plan and mechanisms; clearly a policy commitment guiding municipal operations.

policy_statement: Require energy benchmarking and annual reporting for nonresidential and multifamily buildings ≥20,000 sq ft (SMC 22.920), and make reported data publicly available (2016 update).
source_quote: Adopted in 2010, Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 square feet or larger) to track energy performance and annually report to the City. The policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available to further increase awareness of building energy use and support real estate market transformation.
extraction_rationale: Binding ordinance-based requirement with a defined applicability threshold and annual reporting; includes transparency requirement, making it a concrete policy.

policy_statement: Require commercial buildings ≥50,000 sq ft to conduct building tune-ups to identify and implement low/no-cost O&M improvements for energy and water efficiency (SMC 22.930), with phased compliance beginning in early 2019.
source_quote: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019. A Qualified Tune-Ups Specialist is required to complete the assessment, report to the City, and monitor implementation of operational and maintenance improvements.
extraction_rationale: A binding ordinance with specified building-size threshold and compliance timing; named intervention (tune-ups) with defined purpose.

policy_statement: Commit the City to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners (Resolution 31652).
source_quote: Across the entire commercial building sector, the tune-up mandate is expected to reduce energy use 5-8 percent and GHG emissions by 6-9 percent. To demonstrate leadership, build capacity in the industry, and help reduce compliance costs, the city has committed (Resolution 31652) to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
extraction_rationale: A formal City commitment via resolution that accelerates compliance for City-owned buildings; concrete operational commitment.

policy_statement: Implement the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance for 50,000–100,000 sq ft buildings (DOE grant-funded).
source_quote: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance. The Accelerator is a voluntary program for owners of mid-size buildings, those 50,000-100,000 square feet, to conduct a tune-up that meets the Seattle Building Tune-Up requirements in advance of when their building is required to comply (2020 & 2021).
extraction_rationale: A specific incentives program backed by external funding (grant) with defined eligibility and timing; qualifies as both named intervention and financial instrument.

policy_statement: Convert 200 oil-heated homes per year to electric heat pumps via the Oil Heated Home Conversion incentive program.
source_quote: The Oil Heated Home Conversion program provides incentives for homeowners to replace oil heating systems with clean, efficient, electric heat pumps. By partnering with a heat pump distributor, who provides matching incentives, the program is converting 200 homes per year, while also influencing the home heating market so that electric heat pumps become a preferred heating system.
extraction_rationale: Named incentive program with quantified annual conversions (200 homes per year) and specific technology intervention (electric heat pumps).

policy_statement: Implement Seattle’s commercial energy code, including 2015 SEC criteria addressing emissions by requiring either non-fossil-fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
source_quote: An analysis of the 2012 Seattle Energy Code (SEC) found that commercial buildings meeting our code are, on average, 11 percent more efficient than those designed to the national standard, and the current code is approximately 20 percent more efficient. The 2015 SEC includes criteria that address emissions, by requiring either non-fossil fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
extraction_rationale: A regulatory code with explicit emissions-related compliance pathways; qualifies as a binding mechanism. The quote is partly descriptive but references enforceable code criteria.

policy_statement: Provide expedited permitting (Priority Green) for projects meeting green building certification and performance criteria including 15% better-than-code energy efficiency and water conservation.
source_quote: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections provides expedited permitting for projects achieving green building certification and meeting minimum energy efficiency (15 percent better than code), water conservation, and indoor air quality criteria.
extraction_rationale: A specific administrative incentive (expedited permitting) conditioned on measurable performance thresholds; concrete intervention.

policy_statement: Provide zoning incentives for projects meeting the City’s Green Building Standard, including demonstrating 15% better-than-code energy efficiency (Director’s Rule 20-2017).
source_quote: Multiple zoning incentives are available to catalyze development that is more efficient than code: - Currently, developers can access additional height, floor area, or density In certain zones by meeting the City's Green Building Standard. Director's Rule 20-2017 identifies the requirements, which include - adhering to one of the building industry's green building certification programs and demonstrating that the project is 15 percent more energy efficient than code.
extraction_rationale: A defined zoning-based incentive mechanism with measurable eligibility criteria and a referenced Director’s Rule, qualifying as a concrete policy tool.

policy_statement: Provide additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects via the Living Building Pilot Program if projects meet Living Building Petal Certification and other criteria including no on-site fossil-fuel combustion and 25% better-than-code energy efficiency.
source_quote: The Living Building Pilot Program provides additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects that meet more extensive green building criteria, which includes achieving Living Building Petal Certification (with no on-site combustion of fossil-fuels), plus energy efficiency 25 percent better than code, and greywater and/or rainwater used for all non-potable water needs (e.g. toilet flushing, irrigation).
extraction_rationale: A named pilot program with a numeric cap (up to 20 projects) and explicit fossil fuel and efficiency requirements; a clear zoning incentive policy.

policy_statement: Require City-owned new construction and major renovations to achieve LEED Gold and be 15% more energy efficient than current code (Sustainable Building Policy).
source_quote: In 2011, the City updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings covering new construction and major renovations. In addition to the requirement to achieve LEED Gold certification, projects are expected to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
extraction_rationale: A municipal policy that sets explicit standards (LEED Gold and 15% better than code) for City-owned projects, constituting a binding requirement for City operations.

policy_statement: Achieve a 20% reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 relative to a 2008 baseline (Resource Conservation Management Plan, Resolution 31491).
source_quote: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline). The Office of Sustainability & Environment manages the dedicated funding and works with individual capital departments to prioritize, develop and implement energy efficiency projects.
extraction_rationale: Quantified, time-bound municipal target adopted by resolution; includes dedicated funding management, indicating resource allocation and implementation structure.

policy_statement: Develop and release a strategy to address congestion and transportation emissions through pricing, coupled with investments in expanded transit and electrification in underserved communities.
source_quote: Announce that the City will develop and release a strategy to address congestion and transportation emissions through pricing, coupled with investments in expanded transit and electrification in underserved communities. Research suggests that the most effective strategy for reducing GHG emissions and generating the revenue needed to support transportation alternatives is to put a price on the use of city streets through congestion pricing or some other method of pricing transportation externalities.
extraction_rationale: A concrete commitment to develop and release a pricing strategy (a financial instrument) explicitly tied to reducing transportation emissions and paired with investments; although partly process-oriented, it is anchored to a specific intervention (pricing).

policy_statement: Pass an electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with EV infrastructure.
source_quote: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure. The most cost-effective time to build charging stations and provide the electrical infrastructure for future charging stations is during initial site development.
section_header: ## Electric vehicle readiness ordinance for new construction
extraction_rationale: Explicit time-bound regulatory commitment (pass an ordinance in 2018) requiring EV readiness in parking structures.

policy_statement: Release a map of optimal distribution of EV charging infrastructure in 2018.
source_quote: Release map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018. While Seattle is one of the top US markets for electric vehicles, charging availability and convenience is a significant barrier to widespread adoption.
extraction_rationale: A time-bound deliverable (2018 map) that functions as a concrete planning instrument to guide deployment and investment in charging infrastructure.

policy_statement: Develop recommendations to electrify for-hire fleets, determine a year after which all such vehicles must be electric, and prepare to permit only electric vehicles for for-hire use.
source_quote: The city will work with these stakeholders to create recommendations on how to electrify shared vehicle fleets, determine a year after which all such vehicles must be electric, and prepare for the City to begin permitting only electric vehicles for for-hire use. Work with community and business stakeholders to develop recommendations for making all new forhire vehicles in Seattle electric.
section_header: ## Ride share and taxi fleet electrification
extraction_rationale: A commitment to develop a future regulatory approach (permitting only EVs for for-hire use) and set a compliance year, meeting the specific intervention criterion; some details remain to be determined.

policy_statement: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan by the end of 2018 to accelerate municipal fleet electrification and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
source_quote: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan, by the end of 2018, to accelerate the electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles. Continued fleet electrification requires a shift in the acquisition and fueling of vehicles and vehicle infrastructure.
extraction_rationale: A time-bound commitment (by end of 2018) to create an updated plan with explicit outcomes (accelerate electrification, phase out fossil fuels).

policy_statement: Issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments, with OSE to define scope/method by October 2018.
source_quote: Issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments. The Mayor will direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment to work with departments to define the types of projects to assess, the assessment method, and how to account for the broader costs of carbon, by October 2018.
section_header: ## Assess GHG Emissions Impact of City decisions
extraction_rationale: A binding mechanism (Executive Order) plus a specific implementation deadline (October 2018) for methodology definition; establishes a required climate impact assessment process.

policy_statement: Prioritize 2019 state legislation to enable a tiered residential energy code that cities can adopt.
source_quote: Adopt a tiered state residential energy code that can be adopted by cities. Legislation to allow for tiered energy codes will be a priority for the City of Seattle in the 2019 legislative session.
section_header: ## Washington State tiered residential energy code
extraction_rationale: A concrete legislative agenda commitment with a specified timeframe (2019 session) and defined regulatory change (tiered residential energy code).

policy_statement: Create a 2030 Challenge pilot offering additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovations by 2025 in exchange for deep energy and water cuts and no fossil fuels for heating.
source_quote: Create pilot program offering additional height and floor area incentives for significant upgrades in energy and water use, and transportation efficiency. A new pilot will offer additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovations in urban centers outside the International District. Projects would receive the incentives in exchange for cutting energy and water use well below code, including no use of fossil fuels for heating.
section_header: ## Establish new 2030 Challenge pilot for 20 upgraded, high performing projects by 2025
extraction_rationale: A named pilot program with quantified scale (up to 20 renovations) and explicit performance requirements including no fossil fuels for heating; uses zoning incentives as the mechanism.

policy_statement: Implement incentive-driven performance standards for commercial and multifamily buildings, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030.
source_quote: Provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030. Leading up to 2030, the city will work with building owners through incentives, technical assistance and other programs to help them become voluntary early adopters of the standards, and would then phase in performance requirements beginning in 2030.
section_header: ## Incentive-driven 2030 performance standards for commercial and multifamily buildings
extraction_rationale: Contains an explicit future regulatory backstop (minimum performance standard beginning in 2030) and committed use of incentives/programs; qualifies as a policy package even though some design details are to be developed.

policy_statement: Scale Pay for Performance and pilot Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings.
source_quote: Scale Pay for Performance efforts and pilot an innovative utility program exploring Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings to unlock greater levels of energy efficiency depth at scale. To address the 'hard to reach' energy savings, Seattle City Light is developing programs specifically aimed at enabling greater levels of energy efficiency depth in buildings.
section_header: ## City Light Whole Building Pay for Performance (P4P) Programs
extraction_rationale: A specific utility program commitment with quantified scope (up to 30 buildings) and an incentive-based mechanism, meeting the named intervention/financial instrument criteria.

policy_statement: Double budget allocation for municipal building energy reduction and target a 40% reduction in energy use and carbon emissions in municipal buildings by 2025.
source_quote: Double existing budget allocation for reducing energy in municipal buildings from 2012-2015, with a goal of reducing energy use by 40 percent. Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
section_header: ## Improve municipal building energy efficiency and reduce carbon emissions by 2025
extraction_rationale: Includes explicit resource allocation (double budget) and quantified, time-bound target (40% by 2025) for municipal buildings; clearly qualifies as a policy commitment.

policy_statement: Develop recommendations and a financing/incentive plan to convert 18,000 oil-heated homes to electric heat pumps, including support for low-income residents.
source_quote: OSE will propose recommendations to the mayor aimed at accelerating the transition of 18,000 homes from heating with oil to an electric heat pump, and adequately serving those unable to finance the switch alone. Recommendations will include a financing plan to support low-income residents, as well as a combination of public and private incentives to spur action.
extraction_rationale: Quantified scale (18,000 homes) and commitment to develop financing and incentives; while framed as recommendations, it assigns a specific deliverable to OSE with defined scope.

policy_statement: In 2011, the Mayor and Seattle City Council adopted a community climate protection goal for Seattle to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
source_quote: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council adopted a bold climate protection goal for our community to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
extraction_rationale: Formal adoption via a cited City resolution with a quantified target and deadline.

policy_statement: Seattle City Council adopted Resolution 31757 affirming Seattle's commitment to the Paris Agreement goals and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify actions necessary to do Seattle’s part to limit warming to 1.5°C.
source_quote: In response to the Trump administration's withdrawal of support for the international Paris Climate Agreement last year, the City Council adopted Resolution 31757, affirming Seattle's commitment to the goals established in the Paris Agreement, and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify the actions necessary to do our part to limit warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius.
extraction_rationale: City Council resolution with a directive to a named office tied to an explicit climate objective.

policy_statement: In 2017, Seattle amended the Seattle Electrical Code to require adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and to require construction documents to show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of EV charging stations.
source_quote: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and that construction documents show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of electric vehicles charging stations.
extraction_rationale: Enforceable code amendment specifying EV-readiness requirements for new parking.

policy_statement: Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings 20,000 square feet or larger to track energy performance and annually report to the City; in 2016 the policy was updated to make reported data publicly available.
source_quote: Adopted in 2010, Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 square feet or larger) to track energy performance and annually report to the City. The policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available to further increase awareness of building energy use and support real estate market transformation.
extraction_rationale: Municipal code reporting requirement with defined applicability threshold; includes a transparency update.

policy_statement: Seattle's Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identify low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency, with compliance deadlines phased in by building size beginning in early 2019.
source_quote: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019.
extraction_rationale: Enforceable ordinance with defined size threshold and stated compliance schedule start.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle committed (Resolution 31652) to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
source_quote: To demonstrate leadership, build capacity in the industry, and help reduce compliance costs, the city has committed (Resolution 31652) to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
extraction_rationale: Formal municipal commitment anchored in a cited resolution; exact deadline date is not provided in the excerpt.

policy_statement: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City of Seattle is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance.
source_quote: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance.
extraction_rationale: Program implementation backed by an explicit external funding source and incentive mechanism.

policy_statement: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections provides expedited permitting for projects achieving green building certification and meeting minimum energy efficiency (15 percent better than code), water conservation, and indoor air quality criteria.
source_quote: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections provides expedited permitting for projects achieving green building certification and meeting minimum energy efficiency (15 percent better than code), water conservation, and indoor air quality criteria.
extraction_rationale: An implemented municipal permitting program conditioned on meeting specified performance criteria, including explicit energy efficiency thresholds.

policy_statement: Director's Rule 20-2017 identifies requirements for developers to access additional height, floor area, or density in certain zones by meeting the City's Green Building Standard, including demonstrating that the project is 15 percent more energy efficient than code.
source_quote: Director's Rule 20-2017 identifies the requirements, which include - adhering to one of the building industry's green building certification programs and demonstrating that the project is 15 percent more energy efficient than code.
extraction_rationale: A formal Director's Rule is an adopted instrument specifying requirements for accessing zoning incentives, including an explicit efficiency threshold.

policy_statement: The Living Building Pilot Program provides additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects that meet criteria including Living Building Petal Certification (with no on-site combustion of fossil-fuels) and energy efficiency 25 percent better than code.
source_quote: The Living Building Pilot Program provides additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects that meet more extensive green building criteria, which includes achieving Living Building Petal Certification (with no on-site combustion of fossil-fuels), plus energy efficiency 25 percent better than code, and greywater and/or rainwater used for all non-potable water needs (e.g. toilet flushing, irrigation).
extraction_rationale: This is a defined zoning incentive pilot with a quantified cap (up to 20 projects) and explicit requirements, including a fossil-fuel combustion prohibition and an efficiency threshold.

policy_statement: In 2011, Seattle updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings covering new construction and major renovations to require LEED Gold certification and to expect projects to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
source_quote: In 2011, the City updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings covering new construction and major renovations. In addition to the requirement to achieve LEED Gold certification, projects are expected to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
extraction_rationale: Municipal policy update for City-owned buildings with explicit certification and performance expectations.

policy_statement: In 2013, Seattle adopted the Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491) with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline).
source_quote: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline).
extraction_rationale: Adopted plan via a cited resolution with quantified target, deadline, baseline, and defined municipal scope (City-owned buildings).

policy_statement: The 2015 Seattle Energy Code includes criteria that address emissions by requiring either non-fossil fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
source_quote: The 2015 SEC includes criteria that address emissions, by requiring either non-fossil fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
extraction_rationale: Explicit building code requirement describing compliance options; the section defines this as part of Seattle’s commercial energy code.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light provides an annual grant to Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections to help make possible the work to develop and implement Seattle's advanced energy code.
source_quote: Seattle City Light provides an annual grant to Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections to help make possible the work to develop and implement Seattle's advanced code.
extraction_rationale: Budget/resource commitment (annual grant) by a named City utility to support code development/implementation; no dollar amount specified.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations through the right-of-way charging pilot and expects to launch a residential in-home charging program in the fall of 2018.
source_quote: Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city. The residential program will offer customers access to inhome charging at a manageable monthly cost and is expected to launch in the fall of 2018.
extraction_rationale: Concrete utility implementation actions with a quantified deployment (20 stations) and an expected launch timeframe (fall 2018).

policy_statement: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025.
source_quote: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
extraction_rationale: Quantified municipal operations target with deadline and an identified guiding plan.

policy_statement: Seattle will pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
source_quote: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
section_header: ## Electric vehicle readiness ordinance for new construction
extraction_rationale: Commitment to adopt an ordinance with a stated year (2018) and defined scope (parking structures).

policy_statement: Seattle will release a map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018.
source_quote: Release map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018.
extraction_rationale: Time-bound City commitment to produce a planning instrument for EV charging infrastructure deployment.

policy_statement: Seattle will create a new Green Fleet Action Plan by the end of 2018 to accelerate the electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
source_quote: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan, by the end of 2018, to accelerate the electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
extraction_rationale: Time-bound municipal planning commitment (by end of 2018) with a clear mitigation intent for municipal vehicles.

policy_statement: The Mayor will issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments, and will direct OSE to define the types of projects to assess, the assessment method, and how to account for broader carbon costs by October 2018.
source_quote: Issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments. The Mayor will direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment to work with departments to define the types of projects to assess, the assessment method, and how to account for the broader costs of carbon, by October 2018.
section_header: ## Assess GHG Emissions Impact of City decisions
extraction_rationale: Formal executive action (Executive Order) with an interdepartmental requirement and an implementation milestone (October 2018).

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will make legislation to allow tiered energy codes a priority in the 2019 legislative session.
source_quote: Legislation to allow for tiered energy codes will be a priority for the City of Seattle in the 2019 legislative session.
section_header: ## Washington State tiered residential energy code
extraction_rationale: Explicit City commitment to pursue state legislative change with a specified timeframe (2019 legislative session).

policy_statement: Seattle will provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030.
source_quote: Provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030.
section_header: ## Incentive-driven 2030 performance standards for commercial and multifamily buildings
extraction_rationale: Contains a future regulatory anchor (minimum performance standard beginning in 2030), paired with a program/incentive approach; details are not specified in the quote.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light will scale Pay for Performance efforts and pilot an innovative utility program exploring Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings.
source_quote: Scale Pay for Performance efforts and pilot an innovative utility program exploring Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings to unlock greater levels of energy efficiency depth at scale.
section_header: ## City Light Whole Building Pay for Performance (P4P) Programs
extraction_rationale: Utility program commitment with a quantified pilot scale (up to 30 buildings) and an incentive/service delivery mechanism.

policy_statement: Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, Seattle will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025 and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
source_quote: Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
section_header: ## Improve municipal building energy efficiency and reduce carbon emissions by 2025
extraction_rationale: Combines a budget/resource commitment (doubling budget allocation 2021–2025) with a quantified target and deadline (40% by 2025) for a defined municipal scope (municipal buildings).

policy_statement: Seattle's Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) will propose recommendations to the Mayor aimed at accelerating the transition of 18,000 homes from heating with oil to an electric heat pump.
source_quote: OSE will propose recommendations to the mayor aimed at accelerating the transition of 18,000 homes from heating with oil to an electric heat pump, and adequately serving those unable to finance the switch alone.
extraction_rationale: Commitment by a named City office to deliver a quantified conversion proposal (18,000 homes); no completion deadline is specified in the quote.

policy_statement: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council of Seattle adopted a community-wide climate protection goal for Seattle to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
source_quote: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council adopted a bold climate protection goal for our community to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312). The resulting 2013 Climate Action Plan (CAP) provided a coordinated strategy aimed at reducing GHG emissions while also supporting other community goals, including building vibrant neighborhoods, fostering economic prosperity, and enhancing racial and social justice. The 2013 CAP included a slate of near-term actions - many of which were completed by 2015 - and more broadly defined longer-term strategies to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050.
extraction_rationale: This is an adopted City goal and directive via a named resolution with a quantified target and deadline (carbon neutral by 2050).

policy_statement: Seattle City Council adopted Resolution 31757 affirming Seattle's commitment to the goals established in the Paris Agreement and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify actions necessary for Seattle to do its part to limit warming to 1.5°C.
source_quote: In response to the Trump administration's withdrawal of support for the international Paris Climate Agreement last year, the City Council adopted Resolution 31757, affirming Seattle's commitment to the goals established in the Paris Agreement, and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify the actions necessary to do our part to limit warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius.
extraction_rationale: This is an adopted City Council resolution with a specific directive to a named City office; it is a strong formal-instrument commitment tied to the Paris Agreement and 1.5°C.

policy_statement: In 2017, the City of Seattle amended the Seattle Electrical Code to require that new parking provide adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and require construction documents to show the location of equipment and conduit for future EV charging installation.
source_quote: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and that construction documents show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of electric vehicles charging stations.
extraction_rationale: This is an enforceable code amendment with clearly stated technical requirements for new parking.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light is designing and implementing two EV charging infrastructure pilot programs, including installing 20 public fast charging stations through the right-of-way charging pilot program; the residential program is expected to launch in fall 2018.
source_quote: In response to customers' increasing desire for electric vehicles and to understand how best to manage the impact of vehicle charging on the electrical grid, Seattle City Light is designing and implementing two charging infrastructure pilot programs. The programs will expand public fast charging availability and support residential charging at home. Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city. The residential program will offer customers access to inhome charging at a manageable monthly cost and is expected to launch in the fall of 2018.
extraction_rationale: This is a named agency (Seattle City Light) implementation commitment with a quantified infrastructure deliverable (20 fast chargers) and a stated launch timeline for a program.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
source_quote: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
section_header: Electric vehicle readiness ordinance for new construction
extraction_rationale: This is a clear City legislative commitment with a specified year (2018) and defined scope (parking structures).

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will release a map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018.
source_quote: Release map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018.
extraction_rationale: This is a time-bound City deliverable (map release in 2018) intended to guide charging deployment and investment.

policy_statement: Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings 20,000 square feet or larger to track energy performance and annually report to the City; the policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available.
source_quote: Adopted in 2010, Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 square feet or larger) to track energy performance and annually report to the City. The policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available to further increase awareness of building energy use and support real estate market transformation.
extraction_rationale: This is an enforceable municipal code requirement with defined building size threshold and annual reporting; the 2016 update adds a transparency requirement.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle's Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930), adopted in 2016, requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identify operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency; compliance deadlines are phased in by building size beginning in early 2019, and a Qualified Tune-Ups Specialist must complete the assessment and report to the City.
source_quote: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019.  A Qualified Tune-Ups Specialist is required to complete the assessment, report to the City, and monitor implementation of operational and maintenance improvements.
extraction_rationale: This is an enforceable ordinance requirement with a defined size threshold and stated compliance phase-in start.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle committed via Resolution 31652 to meet the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
source_quote: To demonstrate leadership, build capacity in the industry, and help reduce compliance costs, the city has committed (Resolution 31652) to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
extraction_rationale: This is a formal commitment referenced to a named resolution, specifying an accelerated compliance timeline for City buildings.

policy_statement: With funding from a U.S. Department of Energy grant, the City of Seattle is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program to provide incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance for owners of mid-size buildings (50,000–100,000 square feet).
source_quote: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance. The Accelerator is a voluntary program for owners of mid-size buildings, those 50,000-100,000 square feet, to conduct a tune-up that meets the Seattle Building Tune-Up requirements in advance of when their building is required to comply (2020 & 2021).
extraction_rationale: This is a funded program implementation (DOE grant) that provides incentives (financial instrument) and specifies the eligible building size range and early-compliance timing.

policy_statement: Seattle's 2015 Seattle Energy Code includes criteria that address emissions by requiring either non-fossil-fuel-based heating or more efficient windows for covered commercial buildings (including multifamily buildings four stories and above).
source_quote: The 2015 SEC includes criteria that address emissions, by requiring either non-fossil fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
extraction_rationale: This is a code requirement that explicitly addresses emissions through specified compliance pathways.

policy_statement: In 2011, the City of Seattle updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings to require LEED Gold certification for new construction and major renovations and to expect projects to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
source_quote: In 2011, the City updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings covering new construction and major renovations. In addition to the requirement to achieve LEED Gold certification, projects are expected to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
extraction_rationale: This is a municipal (City-owned) building policy requirement with specified certification and quantified efficiency expectation.

policy_statement: In 2013, the City of Seattle adopted the Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491) with a goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 from a 2008 baseline, with dedicated funding managed by the Office of Sustainability & Environment.
source_quote: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline). The Office of Sustainability & Environment manages the dedicated funding and works with individual capital departments to prioritize, develop and implement energy efficiency projects.
extraction_rationale: This is an adopted plan via resolution with a quantified target, deadline, and baseline; the quote also states dedicated funding as the implementation mechanism.

policy_statement: Seattle's Oil Heated Home Conversion program provides incentives for homeowners to replace oil heating systems with electric heat pumps and is converting 200 homes per year.
source_quote: The Oil Heated Home Conversion program provides incentives for homeowners to replace oil heating systems with clean, efficient, electric heat pumps. By partnering with a heat pump distributor, who provides matching incentives, the program is converting 200 homes per year, while also influencing the home heating market so that electric heat pumps become a preferred heating system.
extraction_rationale: This is an implemented incentive program (financial instrument) with a quantified annual conversion rate.

policy_statement: Seattle's Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, improved efficiency in vehicle use, and vehicle procurement standards.
source_quote: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
extraction_rationale: This is a quantified municipal fleet emissions-reduction target with a deadline and described mechanisms.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will create a new Green Fleet Action Plan by the end of 2018 to accelerate electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
source_quote: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan, by the end of 2018, to accelerate the electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
extraction_rationale: This is a time-bound commitment to create an updated City fleet plan with explicit electrification and fossil fuel phase-out direction.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light will scale Pay for Performance efforts and pilot an innovative utility program exploring Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings to unlock greater levels of energy efficiency depth at scale.
source_quote: Scale Pay for Performance efforts and pilot an innovative utility program exploring Energy Efficiency as a Service in up to 30 buildings to unlock greater levels of energy efficiency depth at scale.
section_header: City Light Whole Building Pay for Performance (P4P) Programs
extraction_rationale: This is a utility program commitment with a quantified pilot scope (up to 30 buildings).

policy_statement: Seattle's Mayor will direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment to work with departments to define the types of projects to assess for GHG emissions, the assessment method, and how to account for broader costs of carbon by October 2018.
source_quote: City policies, plans and major investments have wide reaching and long-term climate impacts.  However, the City lacks a systematic way to assess the GHG emissions associated with major City actions, and to consider the cost of those emissions during the planning process. Creating such a system would help the City make better decisions in the short-term and be prepared for future financial impacts, such as carbon pricing. The Mayor will direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment to work with departments to define the types of projects to assess, the assessment method, and how to account for the broader costs of carbon, by October 2018.
extraction_rationale: This is a mayoral directive with a clear deadline (October 2018) to establish a systematic GHG assessment approach for major City actions.

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will prioritize legislation in the 2019 legislative session to allow for tiered residential energy codes in Washington State.
source_quote: Legislation to allow for tiered energy codes will be a priority for the City of Seattle in the 2019 legislative session.
section_header: Washington State tiered residential energy code
extraction_rationale: This is an explicit City legislative priority with a specified timeframe (2019 session).

policy_statement: The City of Seattle will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025 and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy use and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
source_quote: City-owned buildings comprise four percent of commercial building area of the city. The City will lead the way toward performance standards by aiming to reduce energy and emissions from our own building portfolio. Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
section_header: Improve municipal building energy efficiency and reduce carbon emissions by 2025
extraction_rationale: This is a budget/resource commitment (doubling budget allocation 2021–2025) paired with a quantified municipal performance target and deadline (40% energy and carbon reduction by 2025).
